title: How Many Death Certificates Do You Actually Need?
summary: Nearly every institution you'll deal with wants its own certified copy — knowing roughly how many to order upfront saves a second trip.
category: After a Loss I: The First Weeks
author: HarborCare Circle Editorial
seoTitle: How Many Death Certificates Do You Actually Need? | HarborCare Circle
metaDescription: Nearly every institution you'll deal with wants its own certified copy — knowing roughly how many to order upfront saves a second trip.
whatYouNeedToKnow: Banks, insurance companies, and government agencies each typically require a separate certified copy.
A reasonable starting estimate is 10–15 copies for most estates.
Ordering extra upfront is cheaper than reordering later.

paragraphs: Certified copies are usually required (not photocopies) by banks, life insurance companies, retirement account custodians, Social Security, the DMV, and any other institution formally closing or transferring an account — and most will keep the copy rather than return it.

While the exact number depends on how many accounts and institutions are involved, ordering somewhen around 10–15 copies upfront is a reasonable estimate for many families, since reordering later costs more time than money.

nextSteps: Make a rough list of institutions you'll need to notify.
Order slightly more certified copies than your first estimate.
Ask the funeral home about their process and pricing for ordering copies.
disclaimer: Requirements vary by institution and state — confirm directly with each one whether a certified copy is required.

sources.name: CDC National Center for Health Statistics: Where to Write for Vital Records
